text: The surgeon attempts to access the areas of the abscess and separate the loops of small bowel, navigating through the dense adhesions in the deep pelvis.
The surgeon attempts to access the areas containing the abscess and carefully separates the loops of small bowel, navigating the complex adhesions in the deep pelvis.
The surgeon navigates into the deep pelvis and suspends the uterus with a stitch to access the collection in the area.
The surgeon navigates into the deep pelvis, focusing on the area where the abscess is located.
The surgeon suspends the uterus using a stitch, providing better access to the deep pelvis for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon visually identifies the cavity in the deep pelvis, which is likely the site of the abscess previously noted.
The surgeon exposes a cavity in the deep pelvis, likely related to the previously mentioned abscess.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate small bowel loops that are adherent to the abscess wall in the deep pelvis.
The surgeon observes small bowel loops that are adherent to the abscess wall in the deep pelvis, indicating the need for careful separation.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate the small bowel loops that are adherent to the abscess wall in the deep pelvis.
The surgeon lifts the small bowel out of the pelvis, carefully maneuvering the loops to avoid injury.
The surgeon carefully lifts the small bowel out of the deep pelvic cavity, separating it from any adhesions to the abscess wall.
The surgeon encounters dense adhesions between the small bowel and the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon. An inferior approach is attempted to access the area, which appears less obstructed.
The surgeon encounters adhesions where the small bowel is stuck medially to the sigmoid colon, requiring careful manipulation to separate the structures.
The surgeon approaches the area from an inferior angle, attempting to access the previously identified plane for dissection.
The surgeon has entered the cavity in the deep pelvis, revealing the interior space previously described.
The surgeon has successfully entered the abdominal cavity, revealing the internal structures and organs within the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on the mesenteric surface of the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon, preparing for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon separates the remaining attachments between the terminal ileal mesentery and the sigmoid colon mesentery, aiming to fully visualize the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon detaches the remaining attachments between the terminal ileal mesentery and the sigmoid colon mesentery.
The surgeon attempts to separate tissue to gain a clear view of the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon has freed the small bowel and is now cleaning the pelvic area, likely removing debris or fluid to improve visibility and prepare for further surgical steps.
The surgeon has completed the process of freeing the small bowel, indicating that it is no longer attached to surrounding structures.
The surgeon attempts to clean the surgical field in the pelvic region, focusing on improving visibility and organization of the area.
The surgeon mobilizes the proximal bowel to ensure it reaches the colostomy site, using the left upper quadrant trocar for access.
The surgeon mobilizes the proximal bowel to ensure it reaches the colostomy site, aligning it with the left upper quadrant trocar.
The surgeon prepares to extract the specimen through the colostomy site, which is located in the left upper quadrant.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through a Pfannenstiel incision, visible in the lower abdominal region.
The surgeon mobilizes the proximal bowel to ensure sufficient length, then proceeds to detach the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon over the pelvic brim.
The surgeon detaches the remaining lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon over the pelvic brim after ensuring sufficient proximal length.
The surgeon separates the remaining attachments on the left side of the surgical field, preparing for further mobilization of the proximal bowel.
The surgeon separates the remaining attachments on the left side of the surgical field, preparing to free the sigmoid colon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs mesenteric transection by closely following the bowel wall, avoiding dissection around the superior hemorrhoidal area due to the presence of an abscess cavity.
The surgeon proceeds with the mesenteric transection after completing the lateral mobilization of the colon.
The surgeon carefully hugs the bowel wall while performing the mesenteric transection, ensuring close proximity to the bowel during the procedure.

The surgeon creates a window and proceeds to dissect along the sigmoid colon down to the rectosigmoid junction, ensuring distal transection. The colon is elevated with lateral and medial mobilization, allowing safe dissection without risk to retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery, providing a clear view for further dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the sigmoid colon, progressing towards the rectosigmoid junction and slightly below, in preparation for distal transection.
The surgeon elevates the mobilized sigmoid colon, proceeding with transection while ensuring no injury to the retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon examines the bowel wall, observing significant secondary inflammation in the area, which visually justifies the decision against performing a primary anastomosis.
The surgeon approaches the bowel wall, preparing to perform further surgical actions on the colon.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the rectal mesentery at the site of the planned distal transection, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses an instrument to carefully dissect and remove the rectal mesentery at the planned site for distal transection.
The surgeon inserts a 12mm suprapubic port for extraction through the Pfannenstiel incision. Despite the awkward angle, the surgeon uses a 60mm stapler to successfully divide the rectum.
The surgeon inserts a 12mm port in the suprapubic region, preparing for extraction through the Pfannenstiel incision.
The surgeon adjusts the angle to staple the bowel wall using a 60mm stapler, overcoming the initial awkward positioning to achieve a suitable alignment for stapling.
The surgeon proceeds to divide the rectum at the area of planned distal transection, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the tissue.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the proximal bowel, preparing it for extraction through the Pfannenstiel incision at the end of the procedure.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the proximal bowel, preparing for extraction through the fan and steel incision.
The surgeon marks the rectum with a proline suture, ensuring visibility for future procedures, especially after the uterus is dropped and the small bowel may obscure the view.
The surgeon marks the rectum with a proline suture, preparing for subsequent steps in the procedure.
The surgeon assesses the retracted and withdrawn rectum, considering suturing it to the lateral sidewall to prevent further retraction into the pelvis but decides against it due to excessive tension.
The surgeon sutures the rectum to the lateral sidewall to prevent it from retracting further into the pelvis.
The surgeon irrigates the areas where the abscess was located, then places a drain in the pelvis. The colostomy is brought out through a left upper quadrant site and matured.
The surgeon irrigates the areas affected by the abscess and places a drain in the pelvis. The colostomy is brought out through a left upper quadrant site and matured.
